content:
Software Development

AI

JavaScript

Python

Software Engineering

6 min read
2 hours ago

--

Nobody sits down to write code the way they did five years ago. The tools changed. The scale changed. And honestly, some of the assumptions we all carried into the decade are starting to look pretty wrong.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here’s what the numbers actually say about where software development stands right now — no hype, no predictions, just what’s real.

There are more developers than ever, and it still isn’t enough
The global developer population hit 47.2 million in early 2026, up from 26.9 million in 2021. That’s nearly double in five years, which sounds like a lot — until you look at the other side of the equation.
Demand is growing faster. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics projects 17% employment growth for software developers from 2023 to 2033, which is described as “much faster than average.” Deloitte surveys found that talent acquisition is the top challenge for 50% of executives. The ratio of computer science graduates to open positions sits at roughly 1 to 3.5.
So the field is growing. The shortage is also growing. Both things are true at the same time, and that tension is reshaping everything from salaries to how companies think about building software in the first place.

AI tools are now the norm, not the experiment
In 2023, 70% of professional developers reported using AI tools as part of their development process. By 2026, that number is 81%, according to the Stack Overflow Developer Survey — and among the 19% who don’t use them yet, 14% say they plan to soon.
GitHub Copilot crossed 20 million paid users in 2025, up from 1.8 million in early 2024. Cursor, the AI code editor from Anysphere, crossed $2 billion in annualized recurring revenue in 2025 — less than thirty months after launch, making it one of the fastest-growing software companies in history by that metric. Claude Code reached over $400 million in annualized revenue within months of broad availability.
These aren’t adoption curves anymore. This is infrastructure.
What’s interesting is the gap between how developers feel about AI tools and what the data actually shows. Developers report expecting around 24% productivity gains from AI assistance. One controlled study found that in practice, experienced developers working on real open-source tasks actually slowed down by 19% when using AI tools — because the overhead of reviewing, correcting, and integrating AI output added friction rather than removing it. The tools are genuinely useful. The gains are real but uneven, and they tend to show up more for earlier-career developers than for senior engineers working on complex existing codebases.

Most software projects still fail the basics
Here’s something that doesn’t get enough attention: 31% of software projects are completed on time, within budget, and meeting requirements. That number comes from the Standish CHAOS research, and it hasn’t improved dramatically in years.
83% of projects get derailed by scope creep. 84% of applications find security vulnerabilities after release. Testing covers only 33% of code on average.
This is the unglamorous side of software development that doesn’t make conference talks. The industry is building more software faster than ever, with better tools than ever, and a significant portion of it is still shipped late, over budget, with security holes, and without adequate test coverage.
None of this means the tools are failing. It means the hard parts of software development — scoping, communication, security discipline, test coverage — don’t get easier just because your code editor is smarter.

Python dethroned JavaScript. Sort of.
JavaScript has been the most-used programming language in Stack Overflow’s developer survey for over a decade. In 2025, Python overtook it — with a 7 percentage point jump from 2024, the largest single-year increase for any major language on record.
On GitHub, Python is now the number one language by pull request volume, ending JavaScript’s long run at the top of the platform. Python pull requests grew 48% year over year, driven almost entirely by data science, machine learning, and AI workloads.
At the same time, 62.3% of all developers used JavaScript in the past year. TypeScript overtook Python as the most popular language on GitHub overall in 2025, per the Octoverse report. Rust has the highest admiration score at 72% in the developer survey — meaning developers who use it overwhelmingly want to keep using it.
The cleaner way to read all of this: there is no single “dominant” language anymore. Python owns AI and data. JavaScript and TypeScript own the web and frontend. Rust is winning developer hearts for systems work. The realistic picture is polyglot, and developers who can move between multiple languages are the ones in highest demand. Python skills appear in 43% of job postings as of early 2026; Python is also the language most non-Python developers say they want to learn next.

The low-code wave is real, and it’s coming from outside IT
By 2025, 70% of new business applications are being built using low-code or no-code technologies, according to Gartner. By 2026, 80% of people using low-code tools are expected to be outside traditional IT departments entirely.
The market for low-code platforms is growing at a 32.2% compound annual growth rate — the fastest of any software development category. That’s not just a stat about tools. It’s a stat about who builds software and what that means for professional developers.
It doesn’t mean developers are disappearing. It means the definition of “developer” is expanding, and the work that professional developers do is shifting toward the parts that low-code tools genuinely can’t handle — complex integrations, security architecture, performance at scale, things that break in interesting ways.

The software market itself is enormous and still accelerating
The global software market reached $823.92 billion in 2025. Global IT spending on software is projected to exceed $6 trillion in 2026, growing 9.8% year over year — the largest spending category increase across all IT segments tracked by Gartner.
Enterprise software spending alone reached $1.25 trillion in 2025, up 14.2% from the previous year. Much of that increase is covering higher prices on existing software, not net-new capabilities — a detail worth noting for anyone who thinks enterprise software is mostly a solved problem.
Docker adoption sits at 71.1% among professional developers, the highest penetration figure for any infrastructure tool. React leads frontend frameworks at 46.9% adoption. Node.js holds the top spot for backend at 49.1%. 74% of organizations use hybrid or custom Agile models rather than pure frameworks.

What this actually adds up to
Software development in 2026 is bigger, faster, more AI-assisted, more globally distributed, and more accessible to non-engineers than it has ever been. The market is growing. The developer population is growing. The tools are genuinely better.
And the fundamental challenges — shipping on time, managing scope, writing secure code, testing properly, making good architectural decisions — remain as hard as they ever were.
The most useful frame might be this: the floor has risen significantly. The tools available to a developer today would have seemed remarkable five years ago. But the ceiling has risen too, and the distance between “something that works” and “something that’s well-built, secure, maintainable, and scalable” is if anything wider than it used to be.
That gap is where the interesting work still lives.

Sources

• Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2025 and 2026. https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2025/

• GitHub Octoverse 2025. https://github.blog/news-insights/octoverse/

• SlashData State of the Developer Nation (2026). https://www.slashdata.co/

• U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook: Software Developers. https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/software-developers.htm

• JetBrains State of Developer Ecosystem 2026. https://www.jetbrains.com/lp/devecosystem-2026/

• Flexera State of the Cloud 2025. https://www.flexera.com/blog/cloud/cloud-computing-trends-state-of-the-cloud-report/

• Standish Group CHAOS Research (referenced via Appfire, 2025). https://appfire.com/resources/blog/software-development-statistic

• Keyhole Software — Software Development Statistics 2026. https://keyholesoftware.com/software-development-statistics-2026-market-size-developer-trends-technology-adoption/

• METR Study: Measuring the Impact of Early-2025 AI on Experienced Open-Source Developer Productivity. https://metr.org/blog/2025-07-10-early-2025-ai-experienced-os-dev-study/

• Gartner IT Spending Forecast 2026 (referenced via CompaniesHistory). https://www.companieshistory.com/software-development-market-statistics

• Digital.ai 17th State of Agile Report. https://digital.ai/resource-center/analyst-reports/state-of-agile-report/
